topic: International Trade and the Global Economy
subject: economics

items.q: What is the technical definition of a recession?
items.correct: Two consecutive quarters of negative real GDP growth — a contraction of the economy.
items.misconception: A recession is when prices fall and people lose jobs.
items.random_wrong: A recession is when the stock market crashes.
items.why: Job losses and stock falls often accompany recessions but don't define them. The technical definition is GDP-based. Students conflate symptoms (unemployment) with definition (negative growth).

items.q: What is the difference between GDP and GNP?
items.correct: GDP measures output produced within a country's borders; GNP measures output produced by a country's nationals wherever they are.
items.misconception: GDP and GNP are the same thing with different names.
items.random_wrong: GDP is income and GNP is spending.
items.why: Foreign profits from a UK firm abroad count in UK GNP but not UK GDP. Profits of a Japanese firm in the UK count in UK GDP but Japan's GNP. The distinction matters for globalised economies.

items.q: What is the difference between nominal and real values?
items.correct: Nominal values are measured in current money; real values are adjusted for inflation to allow meaningful comparison over time.
items.misconception: Nominal means estimated and real means accurate.
items.random_wrong: Nominal values are written and real values are spent.
items.why: Both nominal and real values are 'accurate' — they answer different questions. Real values strip out inflation to reveal actual purchasing power changes. Critical for comparing wages or GDP across years.

items.q: Specialisation based on lowest opportunity cost is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Comparative advantage. “Absolute advantage” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A tariff is a tax on:
items.why: It's Imports. “Exports” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did Ricardo publish his theory of comparative advantage?
items.why: Exact answer: 1817 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
